food_subgroup: Prawn and shrimp dishes; spiced curry preparations

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Prawn Do Pyazza. FOODON_03311737 (shrimp dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Indian prawn curry preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Indian prawn curry dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Prawn Do Pyazza is approximately 250 g, comprising around 8–10 medium prawns in a spiced double-onion gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn curry (Indian-style) — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN component data: cooked prawns and Indian curry base
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for prawn baseline
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Prawn Do Pyazza by name. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns (Penaeus monodon) and a standard Indian onion-spice curry base. USDA FoodData Central NDB 15150 (shrimp, cooked) used as cross-reference for protein and fat. Proportions: ~55% prawn, ~30% onion-spice gravy, ~15% oil and aromatics.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seafood and protein-dominant curry dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked shrimp/prawn (GI ~0–25) and low-carbohydrate Indian curry base
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Do Pyazza. Cooked prawns have a negligible glycemic response (GI approximately 0–5) due to near-zero carbohydrate content. The onion-spice curry base contributes modest carbohydrates (predominantly from onion, GI ~10–15). A composite GI of 25 is assigned as a conservative upper-bound estimate for this low-carbohydrate, high-protein dish, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classifications for protein-dominant seafood dishes. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (25 × 4.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.6 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full restaurant serving of Prawn Do Pyazza may contribute approximately 1050 mg sodium (~46% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size accordingly.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (4.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~25)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Do Pyazza is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content, high protein (13.5 g per 100 g), and low glycemic index of approximately 25, resulting in a glycemic load of only 2.6 per serving; it supports blood glucose control when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~135 mg per 100 g) and sodium load from restaurant preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While prawns provide lean protein and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, the relatively high dietary cholesterol (~338 mg per serving) and sodium content warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing oil-reduced preparation and pairing with vegetables is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Do Pyazza (also spelled Prawn Do Pyaza) is a classic North Indian seafood curry in which medium-to-large prawns are cooked in a rich double-onion sauce — the defining characteristic of any 'do pyaza' dish, where onions are added at two stages of cooking to create layered sweetness and depth. The dish begins with a base of onion paste sautéed with ginger, garlic, tomato, and whole spices such as cumin and cardamom, into which the prawns are added and cooked until just tender. A second addition of sliced or fried onions is folded in near the end, providing textural contrast. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spiced, and aromatic, with the natural sweetness of onion balancing the heat of red chilli and the earthiness of coriander and cumin. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indian restaurants such as Zaffron Tandoori and is typically priced as a premium seafood main course. Nutritionally, Prawn Do Pyazza is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with 13.5 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and only 4.2 g carbohydrate. Its glycemic index is very low (~25), making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Prawns are a source of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and iodine. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable for halal diets.
common_user_queries: Is Prawn Do Pyaza good for diabetics?
How many calories in Prawn Do Pyazza?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or HPB entry exists for Prawn Do Pyazza by name. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and a standard Indian onion-spice curry base, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for prawn macros. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database classifications for protein-dominant seafood dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level evidence is strong and the dish composition is well-defined.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for prawn or curry preparations. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible in non-hydrogenated vegetable oil preparations typical of Indian restaurant cooking. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for a restaurant-style Prawn Do Pyazza at approximately 55% prawn, 30% onion-spice gravy, 15% oil and aromatics by weight. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may be significantly lower. GI of 25 is a conservative upper-bound analogous estimate; the true GI is likely lower given the dominant protein and fat content. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values estimated from typical vegetable oil (sunflower or canola) used in Indian restaurant cooking. All -1 sentinel values reflect nutrients not reported in the primary source.
